title: In Silico Logs
text:
My journey into the molecular world began at the University of Shiraz, where I completed my Bachelor’s degree in Biology. During my time there, I developed a deep fascination with the fundamental mechanisms of life, but I also recognized a growing gap: the sheer volume of biological data was outpacing our traditional methods of analysis.


Driven by the desire to decode this complexity, I moved to the University of Bologna to pursue a Master’s in Bioinformatics. Currently in my second year, I have transitioned from observing biological systems to modeling them.



I view myself as an In Silico Biologist. I don’t just run tools; I build and benchmark workflows to ensure biological questions are answered with statistical rigor. My current toolkit includes:

Genomics: Analyzing NGS data (FastQC, BWA, SAMtools) and exploring epigenetic landscapes via DNA methylation arrays.
Machine Learning: Implementing SVMs and Markov Models to predict protein features and functional domains.
Workflow Engineering: Utilizing Snakemake and Bash to create reproducible, scalable research pipelines.




When I’m not optimizing a Snakemake pipeline or exploring the historic porticos of Bologna, I’m usually writing about my latest technical hurdles on my blog. I believe in open science and the power of clear documentation.

title: Thesis & Internship | Guide to Finding Supervisors
text:
Navigating the Internship Search
Erasmus+ Traineeships and Independent Research
Finding a laboratory and a supervisor for your thesis or research stay can feel overwhelming. This guide is designed to help bioinformatics students structure their search, prepare their applications, and identify potential destinations.


1. Funding Options
There are a three different pathways to fund your stay abroad:

i.   You can apply for funding under the standard Erasmus+ traineeship call.


ii.  Alternatively, you can apply for the Tesi all'Estero Fabit which is FaBiT’s study grants specifically designed for Thesis Preparation Abroad.


iii. Sometimes, you can find paid internship positions, although these are naturally quite rare.







Keep in mind that some countries, such as France, are required by law to pay their interns. Because of this, you might be able to secure paid positions.





2. The Time Frame
Timing is critical when applying for research positions.

If you plan to begin your Erasmus+ Traineeship after the third semester, mid-March to April is the right time to start.
For research stays that are not part of any mobility program, you still have some time.
I suggest starting your search now to become familiar with the process. However, September onward is definitely the optimal time to actively apply.







If you are going for the FaBiT study grants for the preparation of the thesis abroad (Tesi all'Estero Fabit), depending on your start date of your internship, the timeframe varies:
    - June/July: If you are planning to start in January or later;
    - December: If your are planning to start in May or later.





3. Application Materials
Before you start emailing professors, ensure your documents are prepared professionally.

You will need an updated CV and a well-written cover letter.
Try to personalize your cover letters specifically for the laboratories that are your top priorities by mentioning their recent projects, published papers, or ongoing research etc. Additionally, explain how the specific methods they use are relevant to your own academic background and experience.
This targeted approach demonstrates that you are genuinely interested and not just blindly sending automated applications.
Depending on where you are applying for, you may need to provide reference letter(s).
Having a professional and active GitHub profile is not strictly required at this stage, but it is expected within the bioinformatics industry and it can make your application stand out.







I plan to write a follow-up post on setting up a professional GitHub profile and what to showcase there. If you would find this helpful, please let me know.





4. Potential Destinations
If you are unsure where to start looking, here are a few solid strategies and institutions to consider:

You can review the winter school webpage at https://www.biocomp.unibo.it/~school20XX/, replacing the “XX” with the specific year you want to check.
There, you will find lists of professors who could potentially become your supervisor. These professors are a great starting point because they are already familiar with the University of Bologna and our master’s program.
In 2026 University of Côte d’Azur took an Intern from our master’s program.
The Pasteur Institute also publicly lists potential projects and laboratories seeking interns under the Erasmus program.
They offer projects related to Bioinformatics and AI, though these mainly focus on infectious diseases.
The Centre for Genomic Regulation (CRG) in Barcelona occasionally offers paid positions and has previously hosted bioinformatics students from Unibo.
Finally, you could look into the Rostlab at the Technische Universität München.



5. Where to look for the positions
There are a lot of ways to search for such positions. Just to give you a lead, consider:

LinkedIn | Simply create a professional LinkedIn profile and start your search; look for specific relevant keywords (i.e:“internship”,“intern”,“stage” etc) among both “Jobs” and “Posts” filters. Turn on notifications for those searches so that you are among the first ones who find out about them.
EMBL offices in the EU.
There are a few societies and associations where you can find job post listings partners with the International Society for Computational Biology (iscb) such as: SFBI,BITS,HSCBB, HBio, FaBI.
The iscb also has an initiative called “Internship Program” under its iscb Student Council structure. This program has a yearly call in around June; check it out.
Finally, you can also contact the professors who are mentioned in the Erasmus+ Studio PDF shared by Prof. Capriotti.


System Note: This is a working document. I will extend this guide with further information in the future.

title: Projects Portfolio
text:
Statistical Models vs. SVM Objective: To benchmark traditional statistical methods against machine learning algorithms for the accurate prediction of signal peptides based on sequence context.
Biological Context: Signal peptides act as crucial molecular “shipping labels” that direct proteins to their correct cellular locations. Accurately predicting these cleavage sites is essential for understanding protein localization and function.
Computational Approach: In this project, we compared two distinct methodologies to evaluate predictive accuracy:

Statistical Baseline

Utilized the classical von Heijne method.
Relied on weight matrices and sequence statistics.

Machine Learning (SVM)

Developed a Support Vector Machine classifier.
Feature Engineering:

Moved beyond raw sequence strings.
Trained on context-based physiochemical features.
Included: hydrophobicity, charge, and side-chain volume.









Features were extracted from amino acid sequences using a sliding-window approach to capture local sequence context.



Tech Stack:

Python & Scikit-learn for SVM implementation and model evaluation.
Bash scripting for initial data wrangling and sequence parsing.
Custom Python scripts for extracting sliding-window physiochemical features.

Future Directions: Building upon the feature-engineered SVM, a Deep Learning implementation of this classifier is currently under active development to capture more complex, non-linear sequence patterns.




Illumina HumanMethylation450 BeadChip (450K Array)
Objective: To identify significant epigenetic signatures and methylation patterns across distinct sample groups using high-throughput array data.
Biological Context: DNA methylation is a key epigenetic mechanism that regulates gene expression without altering the DNA sequence. By analyzing these patterns, we can uncover biological markers associated with specific conditions or developmental stages.
The “In Silico” Pipeline:

Data Preprocessing & QC

Imported raw IDAT files using the minfi package in R.
Conducted quality control checks and normalized data to remove technical variation.

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

Performed Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to visualize global methylation differences between groups.
Generated Heatmaps of the most variable CpG sites to identify clustering patterns.

Differential Methylation Analysis

Applied statistical tests to identify Differentially Methylated Positions (DMPs).
Evaluated biological significance through fold-change and p-value filtering.


Tech Stack:

R & Bioconductor (minfi, limma) for statistical analysis.
ggplot2 & pheatmap for high-quality biological visualizations.


Key Insight: Utilizing PCA allowed us to confirm that the primary source of variance in the dataset was indeed the biological group, validating the experimental design.





Markov Models for Functional Domain Classification
Objective: To accurately annotate Kunitz Domains within a database of protein sequences by leveraging the probabilistic power of Markov Models.
Biological Context: Kunitz domains are potent protease inhibitors found in various proteins (like aprotinin). Because they are small and highly conserved, they serve as excellent candidates for testing sequence-based annotation algorithms.
The “In Silico” Workflow:

Alignment & Profiling

Utilized Structure-Derived Alignments to capture the physical reality of the protein fold.
Extracted conserved residues and gaps to build a representative profile of the Kunitz fold.

Model Architecture

Implemented Markov Models to represent the transition probabilities between amino acids.
Trained the model to distinguish between “Kunitz-like” sequences and background noise.

Classification Task

Scanned a database of sequences to identify and annotate potential Kunitz domains.
Evaluated model performance (Sensitivity vs. Specificity) against known annotations.


Tech Stack:

Bash & Python for sequence parsing and model implementation.
HMMER / Markov Principles for probabilistic sequence searching.


Key Insight: Structure-derived alignments provide a superior baseline for Markov Models compared to simple sequence alignments, as they account for the 3D constraints that govern protein evolution.

title: In Silico Logs
text:
[kian@bioinfo:~]$ whoami
Kian | MSc Bioinformatics Student @ University of Bologna
Welcome to my digital laboratory. I am a biologist transitioning into the computational space, focusing on the intersection of molecular dynamics, machine learning, and reproducible workflows.


Technical Toolbox [System Report]



Category
Tools & Frameworks




Programming
Python, R, Bash, C++


Data Science
Scikit-learn, Tidyverse, ggplot2


Genomics
FastQC, BWA, SAMtools, Picard, IGV


Workflows
Snakemake, Git, Linux (Ubuntu/Debian)






Latest Research & Projects

Protein Informatics: Predicting signal peptides using feature-engineered SVMs.
Epigenetics: Analyzing 450K Methylation Arrays with Bioconductor.
Structural Bio: Annotating Kunitz Domains using Markov Models.

View full Project Portfolio
